subject: ap-chemistry
title: Salicylic Acid: Synthesis, IMFs, & Titration
stimulus:
A student reacts 0.300 g of methyl salicylate ($\text{C}_8\text{H}_8\text{O}_3$, molar mass 152.15 g/mol) with a stoichiometric amount of a strong base. This product is then acidified to produce salicylic acid crystals ($\text{HC}_7\text{H}_5\text{O}_3$, molar mass 138.12 g/mol).

Properties of Salicylic Acid ($\text{HC}_7\text{H}_5\text{O}_3$):
| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Melting point | 159°C |
| Solubility in $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ at 25°C | 2.2 g/L |
| Specific heat capacity | 1.17 J/(g·°C) |
| Heat of fusion | 27.1 kJ/mol |

parts.prompt: For every 1 mole of $\text{C}_8\text{H}_8\text{O}_3$ (molar mass 152.15 g/mol) reactant used, 1 mole of salicylic acid crystals ($\text{HC}_7\text{H}_5\text{O}_3$, molar mass 138.12 g/mol) is produced. Calculate the maximum mass, in grams, of $\text{HC}_7\text{H}_5\text{O}_3$ that could be produced in this reaction.
parts.scoring_notes: $n(\text{C}_8\text{H}_8\text{O}_3) = 0.300\text{ g} / 152.15\text{ g/mol} = 1.971 \times 10^{-3}$ mol. 1:1 mole ratio, so moles of $\text{HC}_7\text{H}_5\text{O}_3 = 1.971 \times 10^{-3}$ mol. Mass = $1.971 \times 10^{-3} \times 138.12 = 0.272$ g.

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated maximum mass of $\text{HC}_7\text{H}_5\text{O}_3$: 1 point

required_elements: $m = 0.300\text{ g} \times \dfrac{1\text{ mol}}{152.15\text{ g}} \times \dfrac{1\text{ mol HC}_7\text{H}_5\text{O}_3}{1\text{ mol C}_8\text{H}_8\text{O}_3} \times \dfrac{138.12\text{ g}}{1\text{ mol}} = 0.272\text{ g}$

wrong_examples: Using a 2:1 or other ratio — the problem states a 1:1 mole relationship.
Using the same molar mass for both — the products and reactants have different molar masses (152.15 vs. 138.12 g/mol).
common_traps: 1:1 molar ratio. Multiply grams of reactant by the ratio of molar masses (product/reactant) = 138.12/152.15. This is a limiting-reagent / theoretical yield calculation.

parts.prompt: The student's experiment results in an 87% yield of dry $\text{HC}_7\text{H}_5\text{O}_3$. The student suggests that some of the $\text{HC}_7\text{H}_5\text{O}_3$ crystals dissolved in the distilled water during the rinsing step. Is the student's claim consistent with the calculated percent yield value? Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Yes, consistent. The solubility of $\text{HC}_7\text{H}_5\text{O}_3$ in water is 2.2 g/L. During rinsing with distilled water, some crystals could dissolve, causing a loss of product and a yield less than 100%. An 87% yield means 13% of the theoretical product was not recovered, which is consistent with dissolution losses during washing.

scoring_points.description: For 'Yes, consistent' with a valid justification citing solubility of the product in water: 1 point

required_elements: $\text{HC}_7\text{H}_5\text{O}_3$ has measurable solubility in water (2.2 g/L), so some crystals could dissolve during rinsing, reducing yield below 100%.
alternatives.correct_example: Yes, the student's claim is consistent. The data table shows that salicylic acid has a solubility of 2.2 g/L in water at 25°C. When the crystals are rinsed with distilled water, some of the product dissolves, reducing the mass recovered. This loss would cause the percent yield to be less than 100%, which is consistent with the 87% yield observed.

wrong_examples: Saying inconsistent — the solubility data directly supports that some product can dissolve during rinsing.
Only citing the 87% yield without explaining why dissolution is a possible cause of product loss.
common_traps: Percent yield < 100% means product was lost. The question is whether the specific proposed explanation (dissolution during rinsing) is chemically plausible given the data. Solubility = 2.2 g/L > 0 confirms the product is water-soluble and can be lost this way.

parts.prompt: Given the physical properties in the table, calculate the quantity of heat that must be absorbed to increase the temperature of a 0.105 g sample of dry $\text{HC}_7\text{H}_5\text{O}_3$ (molar mass 138.12 g/mol) crystals from 25°C to the melting point of 159°C and melt the crystals completely.
parts.scoring_notes: Two steps: (1) Heat the solid from 25°C to 159°C: $q_{heat} = mc\Delta T = (0.105)(1.17)(134) = 16.5$ J. (2) Melt at 159°C: $n = 0.105/138.12 = 7.60 \times 10^{-4}$ mol; $q_{melt} = n \times \Delta H_{fus} = 7.60 \times 10^{-4} \times 27100 = 20.6$ J. Total: $q_{total} = 16.5 + 20.6 = 37.1$ J.

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated value of either $q_{heat}$ or $q_{melt}$: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
$q_{heat} = mc\Delta T = (0.105)(1.17)(134) = 16.5\text{ J}$

OR

$q_{melt} = \dfrac{0.105\text{ g}}{138.12\text{ g/mol}} \times 27100\text{ J/mol} = 20.6\text{ J}$

wrong_examples: Using $\Delta T = 159$°C instead of $159 - 25 = 134$°C — must subtract initial temperature.
Using 27.1 J/mol instead of 27100 J/mol — heat of fusion is given as 27.1 kJ/mol; convert to J/mol.
common_traps: Two separate heat terms: $q_{heat} = mc\Delta T$ (heating the solid to its melting point) and $q_{melt} = n \Delta H_{fus}$ (phase change at constant T). $\Delta H_{fus}$ must be converted from kJ/mol to J/mol to match units.

scoring_points.description: For the correct total heat = sum of both q terms, consistent with c1: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
$q_{total} = 16.5\text{ J} + 20.6\text{ J} = 37.1\text{ J}$

(Accept consistent values based on student's calculation in c1.)

wrong_examples: Reporting only one of the two q values as the total — both heating and melting contributions must be added.
Multiplying instead of adding — the two contributions are additive (sequential processes).
common_traps: Total heat = $q_{heat} + q_{melt}$. These are two distinct processes (temperature rise then phase change) that occur in sequence, so their heats add.

parts.prompt: The same three types of intermolecular forces (London dispersion forces, dipole-dipole interactions, and hydrogen bonding) exist among molecules of each substance. Explain why the melting point of salicylic acid is higher than that of methyl salicylate.
parts.scoring_notes: Both compounds have the same types of IMFs, but salicylic acid (melting point 159°C) has a higher melting point than methyl salicylate (melting point ~−8°C to ~222°C — approximately 223°C is stated in the exam; however, methyl salicylate has melting point ~−8°C, so salicylic acid is higher). The key reason: salicylic acid has an –OH group and a –COOH group, each capable of hydrogen bonding as both H-bond donors and acceptors, leading to stronger and/or more extensive hydrogen-bonding network compared to methyl salicylate, which has an ester group (weaker H-bond acceptor only, no –COOH donor) plus an –OH. More/stronger hydrogen bonding requires more energy to overcome → higher melting point.

scoring_points.description: For a correct explanation citing more or stronger hydrogen bonding in salicylic acid: 1 point

required_elements: Salicylic acid can form more hydrogen bonds per molecule (or stronger hydrogen-bonding interactions) than methyl salicylate.
More energy is required to separate salicylic acid molecules → higher melting point.
alternatives.correct_example: Salicylic acid has a carboxylic acid (–COOH) group that can act as both an H-bond donor and acceptor, in addition to the –OH group. Methyl salicylate has an ester linkage (–COOCH₃) that is a weaker H-bond acceptor and cannot donate H-bonds from that functional group. Salicylic acid therefore forms more and/or stronger hydrogen bonds per molecule. More energy is needed to overcome these interactions, resulting in a higher melting point.

wrong_examples: Citing only molecular size/mass — both compounds have similar sizes; the question specifies the same three IMF types exist.
Saying London dispersion forces are stronger in salicylic acid — since both have the same three IMF types, the differentiating factor must be the extent or strength of H-bonding.
common_traps: When two compounds share the same types of IMFs, the stronger/more numerous interactions lead to higher melting/boiling points. Focus on how the structural differences affect the number and strength of H-bonds.

parts.prompt: Using the information in the graph, estimate the $pK_a$ of $\text{HC}_7\text{H}_5\text{O}_3$.
parts.scoring_notes: The $pK_a$ of a weak acid equals the pH at the half-equivalence point of the titration curve, where half the equivalence-point volume of base has been added and pH = $pK_a$ (Henderson-Hasselbalch: $[\text{HA}] = [\text{A}^-]$). From the titration graph, the half-equivalence point occurs at 5 mL of NaOH (halfway to the equivalence point at 10 mL). The pH at this point reads approximately 3, so $pK_a \approx 3$.

scoring_points.description: For the correct answer read from the titration graph (approximately 3): 1 point

alternatives.correct_example: At the half-equivalence point (5 mL NaOH added), pH = $pK_a$. Reading the graph at 5 mL of NaOH, pH ≈ 3. Therefore $pK_a(\text{HC}_7\text{H}_5\text{O}_3) \approx 3$.

wrong_examples: Reading the pH at the equivalence point as the $pK_a$ — the $pK_a$ is at the half-equivalence point, not the equivalence point.
Reading the initial pH as the $pK_a$ — the initial pH of a weak acid solution is not the $pK_a$.
common_traps: $pK_a$ = pH at the half-equivalence point. For a 20.0 mL weak acid titrated with 0.0200 M NaOH, the equivalence point is at 10 mL. The half-equivalence point is at 5 mL. Read the pH from the graph at 5 mL NaOH.

parts.prompt: When the pH of the titration mixture is 4.00, is there a higher concentration of the weak acid, $\text{HC}_7\text{H}_5\text{O}_3$, or its conjugate base, $\text{C}_7\text{H}_5\text{O}_3^-$, in the flask? Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: The conjugate base $\text{C}_7\text{H}_5\text{O}_3^-$ is at higher concentration. From part (e), $pK_a \approx 3$. Henderson-Hasselbalch: $\text{pH} = pK_a + \log([\text{A}^-]/[\text{HA}])$. At pH = 4.00: $4.00 = 3 + \log([\text{A}^-]/[\text{HA}])$ → $\log([\text{A}^-]/[\text{HA}]) = 1$ → $[\text{A}^-]/[\text{HA}] = 10$. So the conjugate base is at 10× higher concentration. Alternatively: pH > $pK_a$ means the system is past the half-equivalence point, so more than half the acid has been converted to conjugate base.

scoring_points.description: For the correct answer (conjugate base) with valid Henderson-Hasselbalch or $pK_a$ comparison justification, consistent with part (e): 1 point

required_elements: The conjugate base $\text{C}_7\text{H}_5\text{O}_3^-$ is at higher concentration.
Because pH (4.00) > $pK_a$ (~3), or via Henderson-Hasselbalch showing $[\text{A}^-] > [\text{HA}]$.
alternatives.correct_example:
The conjugate base, $\text{C}_7\text{H}_5\text{O}_3^-$, is at higher concentration. Using Henderson-Hasselbalch:
$\text{pH} = pK_a + \log\dfrac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$
$4.00 = 3 + \log\dfrac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$
$\dfrac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]} = 10 > 1$

Therefore $[\text{C}_7\text{H}_5\text{O}_3^-] > [\text{HC}_7\text{H}_5\text{O}_3]$. (Accept: pH > $pK_a$ → majority in deprotonated form.)

wrong_examples: Saying the weak acid predominates because pH = 4 is acidic — acidic pH does not mean the weak acid form predominates; $pK_a$ comparison is required.
Citing the equivalence point instead of the half-equivalence point.
common_traps: When pH < $pK_a$: acid form dominates. When pH > $pK_a$: conjugate base dominates. When pH = $pK_a$: equal concentrations. Here pH = 4 > $pK_a \approx 3$ → conjugate base predominates. This is consistent with being past the half-equivalence point.

parts.prompt: The student researches benzoic acid ($\text{HC}_7\text{H}_5\text{O}_2$) and finds that it has similar properties to salicylic acid ($\text{HC}_7\text{H}_5\text{O}_3$). The $K_a$ for benzoic acid is $6.3 \times 10^{-5}$. Calculate the value of $pK_a$ for benzoic acid.

wrong_examples: Writing $pK_a = \log(6.3 \times 10^{-5}) = -4.20$ — missing the negative sign; $pK_a = -\log K_a$.
common_traps: $pK_a = -\log K_a$. Plug in $K_a = 6.3 \times 10^{-5}$ and evaluate. The answer should be a positive number for a weak acid with $K_a < 1$.

parts.prompt: The student performs a second titration, this time titrating 20.0 mL of a 0.0100 M benzoic acid solution with 0.0200 M NaOH. Sketch the curve that would result from this titration of benzoic acid on the following graph, which already shows the original curve from the titration of 20.0 mL of 0.0100 M salicylic acid. The initial pH of the benzoic acid solution is 3.11.
parts.scoring_notes: The benzoic acid titration curve should: (1) start at pH ≈ 3.11 (higher than salicylic acid start because benzoic acid is weaker — higher $pK_a = 4.20$ vs ~3); pass through pH = 4.20 at 5 mL NaOH (half-equivalence point, consistent with $pK_a$ from part g); (2) reach the equivalence point at the same volume of NaOH (10 mL) as salicylic acid (both have same moles of acid and base concentration); and (3) inflect steeply at 10 mL. The equivalence point pH will be higher than for salicylic acid (weaker acid → more basic conjugate base).
